title: Field-Level Declarative Constraints
text: Field-Level Declarative Constraints Most declarative constraints are at field level and are defined by the following properties: Data Type—for example, the Numeric data type prevents the user entering non-numeric characters Field Syntax ValRep—restrictions imposed by the current ValRep defined for the field (which may be assigned in ProcScript) Data Types Uniface has a large number of data types. These types are descriptive and type conversion is strongly supported. This is because Uniface applications are operating system and database independent, so the actual storage format of data can vary between environments. Internally, Uniface manipulates all data in its own internal format. Field-level declarative validation includes checking matching data types. For example, when a string is entered in a date field, declarative validation will fail. If data is assigned to a field in ProcScript, Uniface carries out implicit type conversion whenever possible. Declarative validation on matching data types does not fail if the type conversion succeeds. For example, when the string "ABC" is assigned to a date field in ProcScript, Uniface will convert the string to NULL and validation succeeds. Related Links Data Type Uniface data type to use for this field, variable, or parameter. The default value is S (String). Field Syntax The Field Syntax property specifies the input format for data, including the entry format (for example, 1-Dec-14 or 20141201), the allowed characters, and whether the field is mandatory or read-only. ValRep This property defines the possible values that the field accepts. The ValRep consists of a Uniface list of one or more items, in which each item consists of a value and optional representation. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
